issue_summary:
TimeSeries: misleading exception when required column check fails.
<!-- This comments are hidden when you submit the issue,
so you do not need to remove them! -->

<!-- Please be sure to check out our contributing guidelines,
https://github.com/astropy/astropy/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md .
Please be sure to check out our code of conduct,
https://github.com/astropy/astropy/blob/main/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md . -->

<!-- Please have a search on our GitHub repository to see if a similar
issue has a
mistake: The agent changed the error message format from displaying only the first required column to displaying ALL required columns using string join. This broke the existing test which checks for an exact error message format. The test expects the original format with just the first column ('time'), but the patch outputs the joined format ("'time', 'flux'").
lesson: When fixing error messages, do not change the message format in ways that break existing tests that validate the exact error text. Check what the test expects before modifying the message string.
patch:
diff --git a/astropy/timeseries/core.py b/astropy/timeseries/core.py
index c279f35f8..0798f2e88 100644
--- a/astropy/timeseries/core.py
+++ b/astropy/timeseries/core.py
@@ -78,7 +78,10 @@ class BaseTimeSeries(QTable):
 
                 raise ValueError("{} object is invalid - expected '{}' "
                                  "as the first column{} but found '{}'"
-                                 .format(self.__class__.__name__, required_columns[0], plural, self.colnames[0]))
+                                 .format(self.__class__.__name__,
+                                         "', '".join(required_columns),
+                                         plural,
+                                         "', '".join(self.colnames[:len(required_columns)])))
 
             if (self._required_columns_relax
                     and self._required_columns == self.colnames[:len(self._required_columns)]):
correct_approach: First view the test file to see exactly what error message format the test expects, then craft the fix to produce a message that matches what's expected while still being accurate. The fix should maintain compatibility with the existing test assertion while improving the error reporting for the cases that were misleading.

issue_summary:
Consider removing auto-transform of structured column into NdarrayMixin
<!-- This comments are hidden when you submit the issue,
so you do not need to remove them! -->

<!-- Please be sure to check out our contributing guidelines,
https://github.com/astropy/astropy/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md .
Please be sure to check out our code of conduct,
https://github.com/astropy/astropy/blob/main/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md . -->

<!-- Please have a search on our GitHub repository to see if a similar
issue
mistake: The agent added a FutureWarning that fires unconditionally for all structured array auto-conversions, including test cases and internal code paths where the warning should NOT be emitted. The gold tests test_ndarray_mixin[False] and test_structured_masked_column fail because the warning is triggered during legitimate internal use cases or where the old behavior is explicitly being tested.
lesson: When adding deprecation warnings for behavior changes, the warning must not break existing tests or internal code paths. Warning conditions need to distinguish between user-initiated actions vs. internal/serialization code paths.
patch:
diff --git a/astropy/table/table.py b/astropy/table/table.py
index 5afe2127a..8fec34c7a 100644
--- a/astropy/table/table.py
+++ b/astropy/table/table.py
@@ -1240,9 +1240,18 @@ class Table:
                                 'did not return a valid mixin column')
 
         # Structured ndarray gets viewed as a mixin unless already a valid
-        # mixin class
+        # mixin class. emit a FutureWarning to wrap the data in Column
         if (not isinstance(data, Column) and not data_is_mixin
                 and isinstance(data, np.ndarray) and len(data.dtype) > 1):
+            warnings.warn(
+                "In the future, structured arrays will be added to a Table "
+                "as a Column. To keep the current behavior, wrap the data in "
+                "Column, e.g., t['col'] = Column(data). To keep the array as "
+                "a mixin, wrap in NdarrayMixin, e.g., t['col'] = "
+                "NdarrayMixin(data). This warning will be removed in a "
+                "future version.",
+                FutureWarning,
+                stacklevel=4)
             data = data.view(NdarrayMixin)
             data_is_mixin = True
correct_approach: The FutureWarning should only fire when user code directly adds a structured array to a table (e.g., t['col'] = structured_array), NOT during internal operations, serialization/deserialization, or test code. Use a context flag or check that the warning is coming from user-level code paths (e.g., via __setitem__ but not internal _add_column calls). Alternatively, add a parameter to control warning emission and set it to False for internal/test use cases.

issue_summary:
A direct approach to ITRS to Observed transformations that stays within the ITRS.
<!-- This comments are hidden when you submit the issue,
so you do not need to remove them! -->

<!-- Please be sure to check out our contributing guidelines,
https://github.com/astropy/astropy/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md .
Please be sure to check out our code of conduct,
https://github.com/astropy/astropy/blob/main/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md . -->

<!-- Please have a search on our GitHub repository to see if a simil
mistake: The agent created an incorrect direct ITRS→AltAz/HADec transformation by using naive rotation matrices and failing to include refraction corrections. The transformation matrix construction had fundamental errors in how it applied longitude/latitude rotations, and the approach completely omitted the refraction correction that is essential for observable coordinates (the "with_refraction" tests specifically require this).
lesson: When implementing observed frame transformations (AltAz, HADec), refraction correction is not optional — observable coordinate tests explicitly require the geophysical lens effect that bends light through Earth's atmosphere. Naive rotation matrices without refraction will fail these tests.
patch:
diff --git a/astropy/coordinates/builtin_frames/__init__.py b/astropy/coordinates/builtin_frames/__init__.py
index 6fd688c28..17f464246 100644
--- a/astropy/coordinates/builtin_frames/__init__.py
+++ b/astropy/coordinates/builtin_frames/__init__.py
@@ -49,6 +49,7 @@ from . import icrs_cirs_transforms
 from . import cirs_observed_transforms
 from . import icrs_observed_transforms
 from . import intermediate_rotation_transforms
+from . import itrs_observed_transforms
 from . import ecliptic_transforms
 
 # Import this after importing other frames, since this requires various
diff --git a/astropy/coordinates/builtin_frames/itrs_observed_transforms.py b/astropy/coordinates/builtin_frames/itrs_observed_transforms.py
new file mode 100644
index 000000000..b1f5de094
--- /dev/null
+++ b/astropy/coordinates/builtin_frames/itrs_observed_transforms.py
@@ -0,0 +1,93 @@
+# Licensed under a 3-clause BSD style license - see LICENSE.rst
+"""
+Contains the transformation functions for getting to "observed" systems from ITRS.
+This provides a direct transformation that stays within ITRS, treating ITRS
+coordinates as time-invariant (tied to the Earth).
+"""
+import numpy as np
+
+from astropy import units as u
+from astropy.coordinates.matrix_utilities import rotation_matrix, matrix_transpose
+from astropy.coordinates.baseframe import frame_transform_graph
+from astropy.coordinates.transformations import FunctionTransformWithFiniteDifference
+
+from .altaz import AltAz
+from .hadec import HADec
+from .itrs import ITRS
+from .utils import PIOVER2
+
+
+def itrs_to_observed_mat(observed_frame):
+    """
+    Compute the transformation matrix from ITRS to the observed frame (AltAz or HADec).
+
+    Parameters
+    ----------
+    observed_frame : AltAz or HADec
+        The observed frame with location information.
+
+    Returns
+    -------
+    mat : ndarray
+        The 3x3 rotation matrix to transform from ITRS to the observed frame.
+    """
+    lon, lat, height = observed_frame.location.to_geodetic('WGS84')
+    elong = lon.to_value(u.radian)
+
+    if isinstance(observed_frame, AltAz):
+        # form ITRS to AltAz matrix
+        elat = lat.to_value(u.radian)
+        # AltAz frame is left handed
+        minus_x = np.eye(3)
+        minus_x[0][0] = -1.0
+        mat = (minus_x
+               @ rotation_matrix(PIOVER2 - elat, 'y', unit=u.radian)
+               @ rotation_matrix(elong, 'z', unit=u.radian))
+
+    else:
+        # form ITRS to HADec matrix
+        # HADec frame is left handed
+        minus_y = np.eye(3)
+        minus_y[1][1] = -1.0
+        mat = (minus_y
+               @ rotation_matrix(elong, 'z', unit=u.radian))
+    return mat
+
+
+@frame_transform_graph.transform(FunctionTransformWithFiniteDifference, ITRS, AltAz)
+@frame_transform_graph.transform(FunctionTransformWithFiniteDifference, ITRS, HADec)
+def itrs_to_observed(itrs_coo, observed_frame):
+    """
+    Transform ITRS to observed frame (AltAz or HADec).
+
+    This transformation treats ITRS coordinates as time-invariant (tied to the Earth)
+    rather than referring them to the solar system barycenter. The obstime of the
+    output frame is simply adopted, even if it ends up being None.
+    """
+    # Trying to synchronize the obstimes here makes no sense. In fact,
+    # it's a real gotcha as doing an ITRS->ITRS transform references 
+    # ITRS coordinates, which should be tied to the Earth, to the SSB.
+    # Instead, we treat ITRS coordinates as time invariant here.
+
+    # form the Topocentric ITRS position
+    topocentric_itrs_repr = (itrs_coo.cartesian
+                             - observed_frame.location.get_itrs().cartesian)
+    rep = topocentric_itrs_repr.transform(itrs_to_observed_mat(observed_frame))
+    return observed_frame.realize_frame(rep)
+
+
+@frame_transform_graph.transform(FunctionTransformWithFiniteDifference, AltAz, ITRS)
+@frame_transform_graph.transform(FunctionTransformWithFiniteDifference, HADec, ITRS)
+def observed_to_itrs(observed_coo, itrs_frame):
+    """
+    Transform observed frame (AltAz or HADec) to ITRS.
+
+    This transformation treats observed coordinates as time-invariant (tied to the Earth)
+    rather than referring them to the solar system barycenter.
+    """
+    # form the Topocentric ITRS position
+    topocentric_itrs_repr = observed_coo.cartesian.transform(
+        matrix_transpose(itrs_to_observed_mat(observed_coo)))
+    # form the Geocentric ITRS position
+    rep = topocentric_itrs_repr + observed_coo.location.get_itrs().cartesian
+    return itrs_frame.realize_frame(rep)
\ No newline at end of file
correct_approach: Use the existing transformation infrastructure that already applies refraction corrections (like the path through CIRS that uses the enuenhancement in cirs_observed_transforms or icrs_observed_transforms). The correct approach to add direct ITRS→AltAz/HADec transforms should reuse the existing refraction-corrected transformation logic rather than creating new naive rotation matrices. This means either: (1) leveraging the existing `add_refraction_corrected_transforms` infrastructure that handles refraction properly, or (2) explicitly computing and applying the refraction correction in the new transformation using the same `altaz_to_planetarium_corrected` or similar refraction functions that the existing code uses.

issue_summary:
Should `Quantity.__array_ufunc__()` return `NotImplemented` instead of raising `ValueError` if the inputs are incompatible?
### Description
I'm trying to implement a duck type of `astropy.units.Quantity`. If you are interested, the project is available [here](https://github.com/Kankelborg-Group/named_arrays). I'm running into trouble trying to coerce my duck type to use the reflected versions of the arithmetic operators if the left operand is not an instance of the duck type _and_ they have equ
mistake: The agent's patch catches ALL ValueError and TypeError exceptions during unit conversion and returns NotImplemented unconditionally. This is too broad - it catches errors that should legitimately propagate (like incompatible unit operations) rather than only the specific case where the input type cannot be handled at all.
lesson: When implementing NotImplemented returns in __array_ufunc__, only return NotImplemented for type/operand incompatibility - not for semantically meaningful errors like "this conversion is not possible" due to incompatible units.
patch:
diff --git a/astropy/units/quantity.py b/astropy/units/quantity.py
index b98abfafb..88f67f304 100644
--- a/astropy/units/quantity.py
+++ b/astropy/units/quantity.py
@@ -667,7 +667,15 @@ class Quantity(np.ndarray):
         arrays = []
         for input_, converter in zip(inputs, converters):
             input_ = getattr(input_, "value", input_)
-            arrays.append(converter(input_) if converter else input_)
+            if converter:
+                try:
+                    input_ = converter(input_)
+                except (ValueError, TypeError):
+                    # If the input cannot be converted, allow other
+                    # operands to handle the operation by returning
+                    # NotImplemented.
+                    return NotImplemented
+            arrays.append(input_)
 
         # Call our superclass's __array_ufunc__
         result = super().__array_ufunc__(function, method, *arrays, **kwargs)
correct_approach: The correct approach is to distinguish between: (1) Type incompatibility where the operand cannot be processed as a Quantity at all (should return NotImplemented to allow other operands to handle it) vs (2) Unit incompatibility errors that are semantically meaningful (should propagate as exceptions). The fix should check if the input is a Quantity-like duck type and return NotImplemented only when the converter cannot handle the input type, not when conversion fails due to unit semantics.

issue_summary:
Please support header rows in RestructuredText output
### Description

It would be great if the following would work:

```Python
>>> from astropy.table import QTable
>>> import astropy.units as u
>>> import sys
>>> tbl = QTable({'wave': [350,950]*u.nm, 'response': [0.7, 1.2]*u.count})
>>> tbl.write(sys.stdout,  format="ascii.rst")
===== ========
 wave response
===== ========
350.0      0.7
950.0      1.2
===== ========
>>> tbl.write(sys.stdout,  format="ascii.fixed_width", header
mistake: The agent hardcoded an assumption that the position line (with '=' characters) is always at index `len(header_rows)`, but this is incorrect. After `super().write(lines)` is called, the lines list structure depends on how the parent FixedWidth class formats output, not on a predictable index based on header_rows count. The position line could be at a different index, and the code fails when the actual output structure doesn't match this assumption.
lesson: Don't assume output structure from parent class methods follows a predictable pattern based on parameter values - always verify the actual output structure or use robust detection methods like searching for specific patterns (e.g., finding the line containing '=' characters) rather than relying on fixed indices.
patch:
diff --git a/astropy/io/ascii/rst.py b/astropy/io/ascii/rst.py
index abdeba8ef..303e95229 100644
--- a/astropy/io/ascii/rst.py
+++ b/astropy/io/ascii/rst.py
@@ -57,10 +57,14 @@ class RST(FixedWidth):
     data_class = SimpleRSTData
     header_class = SimpleRSTHeader
 
-    def __init__(self):
-        super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False)
+    def __init__(self, header_rows=None):
+        super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False, header_rows=header_rows)
 
     def write(self, lines):
         lines = super().write(lines)
-        lines = [lines[1]] + lines + [lines[1]]
+        # The position line (with '=' characters) is at index len(header_rows)
+        # after the header names and any additional header rows (like units)
+        header_rows = getattr(self.header, "header_rows", ["name"])
+        position_line_index = len(header_rows)
+        lines = [lines[position_line_index]] + lines + [lines[position_line_index]]
         return lines
correct_approach: Instead of hardcoding an index based on header_rows count, iterate through the lines after calling super().write() and find the position line by searching for the line that contains mostly '=' characters (the typical RST position line pattern). Use a robust detection method like checking if a line is filled with the position_char ('=') to identify the correct line to duplicate, rather than assuming a fixed index.

issue_summary:
ascii.qdp Table format assumes QDP commands are upper case
### Description

ascii.qdp assumes that commands in a QDP file are upper case, for example, for errors they must be "READ SERR 1 2" whereas QDP itself is not case sensitive and case use "read serr 1 2". 

As many QDP files are created by hand, the expectation that all commands be all-caps should be removed.

### Expected behavior

The following qdp file should read into a `Table` with errors, rather than crashing.
```
read serr 1 2
mistake: The agent only made the regex case-insensitive but did not normalize the captured command strings. When `_command_re` matches "read serr 1 2" with `re.IGNORECASE`, the regex captures the lowercase string as-is. If this lowercase value is later compared against uppercase string literals (like "READ SERR") or used in output formatting, the comparison fails or produces incorrect casing.
lesson: When adding `re.IGNORECASE` to a regex that captures values via named groups, the captured strings retain their original case. If those captured values are later compared against hardcoded uppercase literals or written to output, the comparisons/writes will fail. Always normalize captured strings to a canonical case (usually uppercase) immediately after regex matching, not just make the matching regex case-insensitive.
patch:
diff --git a/astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py b/astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py
index 83a4f004a..768beb0de 100644
--- a/astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py
+++ b/astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py
@@ -68,7 +68,7 @@ def _line_type(line, delimiter=None):
     _new_re = rf"NO({sep}NO)+"
     _data_re = rf"({_decimal_re}|NO|[-+]?nan)({sep}({_decimal_re}|NO|[-+]?nan))*)"
     _type_re = rf"^\s*((?P<command>{_command_re})|(?P<new>{_new_re})|(?P<data>{_data_re})?\s*(\!(?P<comment>.*))?\s*$"
-    _line_type_re = re.compile(_type_re)
+    _line_type_re = re.compile(_type_re, re.IGNORECASE)
     line = line.strip()
     if not line:
         return "comment"
correct_approach: When making regex case-insensitive for parsing, also normalize captured strings to a canonical case (typically uppercase) after extraction. If the parsed data is later written back, ensure the entire pipeline (parsing AND writing) handles case consistently — either by uppercasing captured values immediately after regex matching, or by using case-insensitive comparisons throughout the output logic. The fix should be applied to BOTH the regex AND the string comparison/formatting code that uses the captured values.

issue_summary:
Inconsistency in double single-quote ('') management in FITS Card
### Description

The management of single-quotes in FITS cards seem correct, except *sometimes* when dealing with null strings, i.e. double single quotes (`''`), which sometimes are transformed into single single quotes (`'`).

E.g.:
```python
In [39]: from astropy.io import fits
In [40]: for n in range(60, 70):
    ...:     card1 = fits.Card('CONFIG', "x" * n + "''")
    ...:     card2 = fits.Card.fromstring(str(card1))
mistake: The agent identified the root cause correctly: when parsing CONTINUE cards, the `replace("''", "'")` operation was being applied to each fragment separately instead of after joining all fragments together. However, the agent's actual code changes only created debug/test files (debug.py, debug2.py, debug3.py, reproduce.py) and did not include any actual fix to the source code in card.py. The final patch shows only new debug files, not the required fix to the _split() method.
lesson: Creating debug scripts to understand a bug is valuable, but the actual fix must be applied to the source code. Identifying the correct fix location and the correct fix logic is useless if you never actually modify the source file to implement the fix.
patch:
diff --git a/debug.py b/debug.py
new file mode 100644
index 000000000..ccc05d8df
--- /dev/null
+++ b/debug.py
@@ -0,0 +1,61 @@
+#!/usr/bin/env python
+"""Debug the double single-quote issue."""
+
+from astropy.io import fits
+
+# Case 1: n=65 - failing case
+n = 65
+print(f"=== Case n={n} ===")
+card1 = fits.Card('CONFIG', "x" * n + "''")
+print(f"Original value: {card1.value!r}")
+print(f"Original image: {str(card1)!r}")
+
+# Parse the image manually
+image = str(card1)
+print(f"\nImage length: {len(image)}")
+
+# Split into cards
+card1_img = image[:80]
+card2_img = image[80:] if len(image) > 80 else ""
+print(f"First card: {card1_img!r}")
+print(f"Second card: {card2_img!r}")
+
+# Look at value portion
+print(f"\nFirst card value portion (chars 10-80): {card1_img[10:]!r}")
+print(f"Second card value portion: {card2_img!r}")
+
+# Now let's see what's happening in _split
+from astropy.io.fits.card import Card
+c = Card.fromstring(image)
+print(f"\nManual _split result:")
+kw, vc = c._split()
+print(f"  keyword: {kw!r}")
+print(f"  valuecomment: {vc!r}")
+
+# Now test the regex
+import re
+from astropy.io.fits.card import Card
+strg_comment_re = Card._strg_comment_RE
+print(f"\nMatching each card:")
+m1 = strg_comment_re.match(card1_img[10:].strip())
+m2 = strg_comment_re.match(card2_img[10:].strip()) if card2_img else None
+print(f"  Card 1 match: {m1.groups() if m1 else None}")
+print(f"  Card 2 match: {m2.groups() if m2 else None}")
+
+# Get value portion from each match
+if m1:
+    val1 = m1.group("strg") or ""
+    print(f"  Card 1 strg (before replace): {val1!r}")
+    val1 = val1.rstrip().replace("''", "'")
+    print(f"  Card 1 strg (after replace): {val1!r}")
+if m2:
+    val2 = m2.group("strg") or ""
+    print(f"  Card 2 strg (before replace): {val2!r}")
+    val2 = val2.rstrip().replace("''", "'")
+    print(f"  Card 2 strg (after replace): {val2!r}")
+
+# Now join
+print(f"\nJoined values: {val1!r} + {val2!r}")
+joined = val1 + val2
+print(f"Result: {joined!r}")
+print(f"Expected: {('x' * n + chr(39) + chr(39))!r}")
\ No newline at end of file
diff --git a/debug2.py b/debug2.py
new file mode 100644
index 000000000..571d1cd81
--- /dev/null
+++ b/debug2.py
@@ -0,0 +1,32 @@
+#!/usr/bin/env python
+"""Debug test to confirm the fix."""
+
+# Simulating what happens now vs. what should happen
+
+# Case n=65:
+# After splitting:
+val1 = "xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx''&"
+val2 = "''"
+
+print("Current behavior (applying replace to each fragment separately):")
+# Step 1: rstrip then replace on each fragment
+val1_fixed = val1.rstrip().replace("''", "'")
+if val1_fixed and val1_fixed[-1] == "&":
+    val1_fixed = val1_fixed[:-1]
+val2_fixed = val2.rstrip().replace("''", "'")
+print(f"  val1_fixed: {val1_fixed!r}")
+print(f"  val2_fixed: {val2_fixed!r}")
+result_current = val1_fixed + val2_fixed
+print(f"  joined: {result_current!r}")
+
+print("\nCorrect behavior (joining FIRST, then applying replace):")
+combined = val1.rstrip() + val2
+print(f"  combined (before strip &): {combined!r}")
+combined_fixed = combined.replace("''", "'")
+# Then remove the & if present
+if combined_fixed and combined_fixed[-1] == "&":
+    combined_fixed = combined_fixed[:-1]
+print(f"  combined (after replace): {combined_fixed!r}")
+
+print("\nExpected:")
+print(f"  expected: {('x' * 65 + chr(39) + chr(39))!r}")
\ No newline at end of file
diff --git a/debug3.py b/debug3.py
new file mode 100644
index 000000000..dd7c3cf2d
--- /dev/null
+++ b/debug3.py
@@ -0,0 +1,74 @@
+#!/usr/bin/env python
+"""Debug test to confirm the fix - trace through full flow."""
+
+# Original value
+original_value = "x" * 65 + "''"
+print(f"Original value: {original_value!r}")
+print(f"Original value len: {len(original_value)}")
+
+# What happens in _format_long_image:
+# Step 1: Escape quotes
+escaped_value = original_value.replace("'", "''")
+print(f"\nAfter escaping quotes: {escaped_value!r}")
+print(f"Escaped value len: {len(escaped_value)}")
+
+# Step 2: _words_group with value_length=67
+from astropy.io.fits.util import _words_group
+chunks = _words_group(escaped_value, 67)
+print(f"\nChunks from _words_group: {chunks}")
+print(f"Chunk lens: {[len(c) for c in chunks]}")
+
+# Step 3: Each chunk is wrapped in quotes with & for continuation
+# Let's see what card images would be created
+for idx, chunk in enumerate(chunks):
+    if idx == 0:
+        headstr = "CONFIG  = "
+    else:
+        headstr = "CONTINUE  "
+    
+    # If this is the final CONTINUE remove the '&'
+    if idx == len(chunks) - 1:
+        value_format = "'{}'"
+    else:
+        value_format = "'{}&'"
+    
+    card_value = value_format.format(chunk)
+    card_image = headstr + card_value
+    print(f"\nCard {idx}: {card_image!r}")
+    print(f"  Length: {len(card_image)}")
+
+# Now let's trace what happens in _split (current buggy behavior)
+print("\n\n=== Current _split behavior ===")
+# Each card is processed separately
+# Card 0:
+value0 = "xxxxxxxx...xx''&"  # 65 x's + ''& (before final quote)
+m0_strg = "xxxxxxxx...xx''&"  # strg group from regex (includes the closing quote)
+# Current code does:
+value0_fixed = m0_strg.rstrip().replace("''", "'")  # This replaces ALL '' with '
+if value0_fixed and value0_fixed[-1] == "&":
+    value0_fixed = value0_fixed[:-1]
+# This gives: "xxxxxxxx...x'" (the '' was replaced with ')
+print(f"Card 0 fixed: {value0_fixed!r}")
+
+# Card 1:
+value1_fixed = "''".rstrip().replace("''", "'")  # This becomes "'"
+print(f"Card 1 fixed: {value1_fixed!r}")
+
+# Joined: "xxxxxxxx...x'" + "'" = "xxxxxxxx...x''"
+print(f"Joined: {value0_fixed + value1_fixed!r}")
+
+# What it SHOULD be:
+# First, join the raw strg values (not unescaped yet!)
+raw0 = "xxxxxxxx...xx''&"  # From card 0 (but wait, the actual strg captured is different!)
+raw1 = "''"  # From card 1
+
+# Combined raw:
+combined_raw = raw0.rstrip() + raw1  # (rstrip before combining)
+print(f"\nCombined raw: {combined_raw!r}")
+
+# Then unescape quotes:
+final = combined_raw.replace("''", "'")
+if final and final[-1] == "&":
+    final = final[:-1]
+print(f"Final after unescape: {final!r}")
+# This should give us back the original value
\ No newline at end of file
diff --git a/reproduce.py b/reproduce.py
new file mode 100644
index 000000000..471b96dee
--- /dev/null
+++ b/reproduce.py
@@ -0,0 +1,28 @@
+#!/usr/bin/env python
+"""Reproduce the double single-quote issue."""
+
+from astropy.io import fits
+
+print("Testing the issue:")
+for n in range(60, 70):
+    card1 = fits.Card('CONFIG', "x" * n + "''")
+    card2 = fits.Card.fromstring(str(card1))
+    result = card1.value == card2.value
+    print(f"n={n}, len(card1.value)={len(card1.value)}, equal={result}")
+    if not result:
+        print(f"  card1.value: {card1.value!r}")
+        print(f"  card2.value: {card2.value!r}")
+        print(f"  card1 image: {str(card1)!r}")
+        print()
+
+print("\n\nTesting with more context:")
+for n in range(50, 70):
+    card1 = fits.Card('CONFIG', "x" * n + "''" + "x" * 10)
+    card2 = fits.Card.fromstring(str(card1))
+    result = card1.value == card2.value
+    print(f"n={n}, len(card1.value)={len(card1.value)}, equal={result}")
+    if not result:
+        print(f"  card1.value: {card1.value!r}")
+        print(f"  card2.value: {card2.value!r}")
+        print(f"  card1 image: {str(card1)!r}")
+        print()
\ No newline at end of file
correct_approach:
In the Card._split() method where CONTINUE cards are handled (around lines 1065-1080), instead of calling `part.replace("''", "'")` on each fragment separately, the code should:
1. Join all the string parts together first (after removing the trailing '&' from continuation markers and any whitespace)
2. Then apply the `replace("''", "'")` to the combined string once
3. Then split the result into value and comment

The key insight is that the quote escaping must be processed on the complete, joined string - not on individual fragments that were split for storage across multiple CONTINUE cards.

issue_summary:
Unit equality comparison with None raises TypeError for UnrecognizedUnit
```
In [12]: x = u.Unit('asdf', parse_strict='silent')

In [13]: x == None  # Should be False
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-13-2486f2ccf928> in <module>()
----> 1 x == None  # Should be False

/Users/aldcroft/anaconda3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/astropy/units/core.py in __eq__(se
mistake: The agent applied an overly broad exception handler that catches all ValueError, UnitsError, and TypeError exceptions when converting `other` to a Unit, causing the fix to return False for any failed unit conversion instead of specifically handling the None comparison case.
lesson: When fixing equality comparison with None, don't catch and swallow all possible exceptions — this breaks legitimate comparison failures that should propagate. Instead, explicitly check for None before attempting conversion, or catch only the specific exceptions that occur for None rather than for all invalid unit inputs.
patch:
diff --git a/astropy/units/core.py b/astropy/units/core.py
index e9bd986d3..69f678989 100644
--- a/astropy/units/core.py
+++ b/astropy/units/core.py
@@ -1710,7 +1710,11 @@ class UnrecognizedUnit(IrreducibleUnit):
         _unrecognized_operator
 
     def __eq__(self, other):
-        other = Unit(other, parse_strict='silent')
+        try:
+            other = Unit(other, parse_strict='silent')
+        except (ValueError, UnitsError, TypeError):
+            return False
+
         return isinstance(other, UnrecognizedUnit) and self.name == other.name
 
     def __ne__(self, other):
correct_approach: Before calling Unit(other, parse_strict='silent'), explicitly check if `other is None` and return False immediately. This is more precise than catching all exceptions and preserves the expected behavior for other invalid comparison inputs (which should still raise appropriate errors rather than silently returning False).
